summary: The post argues that enterprise software buying has shifted from buyer–user mismatch to a new dynamic where popularity, taste, and social proof drive adoption more than product quality. As consumerization gave way to memeification, software now competes in a fashion-like market where attention and celebrity can outweigh marginal technical superiority.
money_quotes: If you have to use Jira, you hate it. If you don’t have to use Jira, you buy it.
Products being bought and sold by backroom negotiation is a form of market failure. Products being chosen on their own merits is pure and efficient.
We do not like things from first principles. We like them because other people like them.
Our desires are memetic; our social proof is gossip; our interests are compelled.
Because, for as much as Silicon Valley celebrates the builders, our economy is content and our opinions come from others' upvotes.
key_insight: Enterprise software is increasingly bought like consumer fashion: attention and status can beat product quality, making meme-driven popularity a core competitive advantage.
